                            "text": "     1\t---\n     2\tname: file-reading\n     3\tdescription: \"Use this skill when a file has been uploaded but its content is NOT in your context — only its path at /mnt/user-data/uploads/ is listed in an uploaded_files block. This skill is a router: it tells you which tool to use for each file type (pdf, docx, xlsx, csv, json, images, archives, ebooks) so you read the right amount the right way instead of blindly running cat on a binary. Triggers: any mention of /mnt/user-data/uploads/, an uploaded_files section, a file_path tag, or a user asking about an uploaded file you have not yet read. Do NOT use this skill if the file content is already visible in your context inside a documents block — you already have it.\"\n     4\tcompatibility: \"claude.ai, Claude Desktop, Cowork — any surface where uploads land at /mnt/user-data/uploads/\"\n     5\tlicense: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms\n     6\t---\n     7\t\n     8\t# Reading Uploaded Files\n     9\t\n    10\t## Why this skill exists\n    11\t\n    12\tWhen a user uploads a file in claude.ai, Claude Desktop, or Cowork,\n    13\tthe file is written to `/mnt/user-data/uploads/<filename>` and you are told the path\n    14\tin an `<uploaded_files>` block. **The content is not in your context.**\n    15\tYou must go read it.\n    16\t\n    17\tThe naive thing — `cat /mnt/user-data/uploads/whatever` — is wrong for\n    18\tmost files:\n    19\t\n    20\t- On a PDF it prints binary garbage.\n    21\t- On a 100MB CSV it floods your context with rows you will never use.\n    22\t- On a DOCX it prints the raw ZIP bytes.\n    23\t- On an image it does nothing useful at all.\n    24\t\n    25\tThis skill tells you the right first move for each type, and when to\n    26\thand off to a deeper skill.\n    27\t\n    28\t## General protocol\n    29\t\n    30\t1. **Look at the extension.** That is your dispatch key.\n    31\t2. **Stat before you read.** Large files need sampling, not slurping.\n    32\t   ```bash\n    33\t   stat -c '%s bytes, %y' /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf\n    34\t   file /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf\n    35\t   ```\n    36\t3. **Read just enough to answer the user's question.** If they asked\n    37\t   \"how many rows are in this CSV\", don't load the whole thing into\n    38\t   pandas — `wc -l` gives a fast approximation (it counts newlines,\n    39\t   not CSV records, so it may over-count if quoted fields contain\n    40\t   embedded newlines).\n    41\t4. **If a dedicated skill exists, go read it.** The table below tells\n    42\t   you when. The dedicated skills cover editing, creating, and advanced\n    43\t   operations that this skill does not.\n    44\t\n    45\t## `extract-text`\n    46\t\n    47\tFor docx, odt, epub, xlsx, pptx, rtf, and ipynb the first move is\n    48\t`extract-text <file>`. It emits markdown for docx/odt/epub (headings,\n    49\tbold, lists, links, tables), tab-separated rows under `## Sheet:`\n    50\theaders for xlsx, text under `## Slide N` headers for pptx, fenced\n    51\tcode cells for ipynb, and plain text for rtf. Pass `--format <fmt>`\n    52\twhen the extension is wrong or absent (e.g., `--format xlsx` on an\n    53\t`.xlsm`). If it errors on a file, `pandoc <file> -t plain` is a\n    54\tfallback; for xlsx/pptx, fall back to the dedicated skill's\n    55\tPython-based approach (openpyxl / python-pptx).\n    56\t\n    57\t## Dispatch table\n    58\t\n    59\t| Extension                         | First move                                           | Dedicated skill                           |\n    60\t| --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |\n    61\t| `.pdf`                            | Content inventory (see PDF section)                  | `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md` |\n    62\t| `.docx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |\n    63\t| `.doc` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.docx` first                             | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |\n    64\t| `.xlsx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |\n    65\t| `.xlsm`                           | `extract-text --format xlsx`                         | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |\n    66\t| `.xls` (legacy)                   | `pd.read_excel(engine=\"xlrd\")` — openpyxl rejects it | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |\n    67\t| `.ods`                            | `pd.read_excel(engine=\"odf\")` — openpyxl rejects it  | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |\n    68\t| `.pptx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |\n    69\t| `.ppt` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.pptx` first                             | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |\n    70\t| `.csv`, `.tsv`                    | `pandas` with `nrows`                                | — (below)                                 |\n    71\t| `.json`, `.jsonl`                 | `jq` for structure                                   | — (below)                                 |\n    72\t| `.jpg`, `.png`, `.gif`, `.webp`   | Already in your context as vision input              | — (below)                                 |\n    73\t| `.zip`, `.tar`, `.tar.gz`         | List contents, do **not** auto-extract               | — (below)                                 |\n    74\t| `.gz` (single file)               | `zcat \\| head` — no manifest to list                 | — (below)                                 |\n    75\t| `.epub`, `.odt`                   | `extract-text`                                       | — (below)                                 |\n    76\t| `.rtf`                            | `extract-text`                                       | — (below)                                 |\n    77\t| `.ipynb`                          | `extract-text`                                       | — (below)                                 |\n    78\t| `.txt`, `.md`, `.log`, code files | `wc -c` then `head` or full `cat`                    | — (below)                                 |\n    79\t| Unknown                           | `file` then decide                                   | —                                         |\n    80\t\n    81\t---\n    82\t\n    83\t## PDF\n    84\t\n    85\t**Never** `cat` a PDF — it prints binary garbage.\n    86\t\n    87\tQuick first move — get the page count and check if text is extractable:\n    88\t\n    89\t```bash\n    90\tpdfinfo /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf\n    91\tpdftotext -f 1 -l 1 /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf - | head -20\n    92\t```\n    93\t\n    94\tThen peek at the text content:\n    95\t\n    96\t```python\n    97\tfrom pypdf import PdfReader\n    98\tr = PdfReader(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf\")\n    99\tprint(f\"{len(r.pages)} pages\")\n   100\tprint(r.pages[0].extract_text()[:2000])\n   101\t```\n   102\t\n   103\tFor anything beyond a quick peek — figures, tables, attachments,\n   104\tforms, scanned PDFs, visual inspection, or choosing a reading strategy\n   105\t— go read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md`. It covers\n   106\tcontent inventory, text extraction vs. page rasterization, embedded\n   107\tcontent extraction, and document-type-aware reading strategies.\n   108\t\n   109\tFor PDF form filling, creation, merging, splitting, or watermarking,\n   110\tgo read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf/SKILL.md`.\n   111\t\n   112\t---\n   113\t\n   114\t## DOCX / DOC\n   115\t\n   116\tThe `docx` skill covers editing, creating, tracked changes, images.\n   117\tRead it if you need any of those. For a quick look:\n   118\t\n   119\t```bash\n   120\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/memo.docx | head -200\n   121\t```\n   122\t\n   123\tLegacy `.doc` (not `.docx`) must be converted first — see the `docx`\n   124\tskill.\n   125\t\n   126\t---\n   127\t\n   128\t## XLSX / XLS / spreadsheets\n   129\t\n   130\tThe `xlsx` skill covers formulas, formatting, charts, creating. Read\n   131\tit if you need any of those. For a quick look at an `.xlsx`:\n   132\t\n   133\t```bash\n   134\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.xlsx | head -100\n   135\t```\n   136\t\n   137\tFor `.xlsm`, add `--format xlsx` (same zip structure; only the\n   138\textension differs). When you need a structured preview in Python:\n   139\t\n   140\t```python\n   141\tfrom openpyxl import load_workbook\n   142\twb = load_workbook(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.xlsx\", read_only=True)\n   143\tprint(\"Sheets:\", wb.sheetnames)\n   144\tws = wb.active\n   145\tfor row in ws.iter_rows(max_row=5, values_only=True):\n   146\t    print(row)\n   147\t```\n   148\t\n   149\t`read_only=True` matters — without it, openpyxl loads the entire\n   150\tworkbook into memory, which breaks on large files. Do not trust\n   151\t`ws.max_row` in read-only mode: many non-Excel writers omit the\n   152\tdimension record, so it comes back `None` or wrong. If you need a row\n   153\tcount, iterate or use pandas.\n   154\t\n   155\t**Legacy `.xls`** — openpyxl raises `InvalidFileException`. Use:\n   156\t\n   157\t```python\n   158\timport pandas as pd\n   159\tdf = pd.read_excel(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/old.xls\", engine=\"xlrd\", nrows=5)\n   160\t```\n   161\t\n   162\t**`.ods` (OpenDocument)** — openpyxl also rejects this. Use:\n   163\t\n   164\t```python\n   165\timport pandas as pd\n   166\tdf = pd.read_excel(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.ods\", engine=\"odf\", nrows=5)\n   167\t```\n   168\t\n   169\t---\n   170\t\n   171\t## PPTX\n   172\t\n   173\t```bash\n   174\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/deck.pptx | head -200\n   175\t```\n   176\t\n   177\t**Legacy `.ppt`** — convert to `.pptx` first via LibreOffice; see\n   178\t`/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md` for the sandbox-safe\n   179\t`scripts/office/soffice.py` wrapper (bare `soffice` hangs here because\n   180\tthe seccomp filter blocks the `AF_UNIX` sockets LibreOffice uses for\n   181\tinstance management).\n   182\t\n   183\tFor anything beyond reading, go to `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`.\n   184\t\n   185\t---\n   186\t\n   187\t## CSV / TSV\n   188\t\n   189\t**Do not** `cat` or `head` these blindly. A CSV with a 50KB quoted cell\n   190\tin row 1 will wreck your `head -5`. Use pandas with `nrows`:\n   191\t\n   192\t```python\n   193\timport pandas as pd\n   194\tdf = pd.read_csv(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv\", nrows=5)\n   195\tprint(df)\n   196\tprint()\n   197\tprint(df.dtypes)\n   198\t```\n   199\t\n   200\tApproximate row count without loading (over-counts if the file has\n   201\tRFC-4180 quoted newlines — the same quoted-cell case this section\n   202\twarned about above):\n   203\t\n   204\t```bash\n   205\twc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv\n   206\t```\n   207\t\n   208\tFull analysis only after you know the shape:\n   209\t\n   210\t```python\n   211\tdf = pd.read_csv(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv\")\n   212\tprint(df.describe())\n   213\t```\n   214\t\n   215\tTSV: same, with `sep=\"\\t\"`.\n   216\t\n   217\t---\n   218\t\n   219\t## JSON / JSONL\n   220\t\n   221\tStructure first, content second:\n   222\t\n   223\t```bash\n   224\tjq 'type' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json\n   225\tjq 'if type == \"array\" then length elif type == \"object\" then keys else . end' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json\n   226\t```\n   227\t\n   228\t(`keys` errors on scalar JSON roots — a bare `\"hello\"` or `42` is valid\n   229\tJSON per RFC 7159 — so guard the branch.)\n   230\t\n   231\tThen drill into what the user actually asked about.\n   232\t\n   233\tJSONL (one object per line) — do **not** `jq` the whole file; work line\n   234\tby line:\n   235\t\n   236\t```bash\n   237\thead -3 /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl | jq .\n   238\twc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl\n   239\t```\n   240\t\n   241\t---\n   242\t\n   243\t## Images (JPG / PNG / GIF / WEBP)\n   244\t\n   245\t**You can already see uploaded images.** They are injected into your\n   246\tcontext as vision inputs alongside the `<uploaded_files>` pointer. You\n   247\tdo not need to read them from disk to describe them.\n   248\t\n   249\tThe disk copy is only needed if you are going to **process** the image\n   250\tprogrammatically:\n   251\t\n   252\t```python\n   253\tfrom PIL import Image\n   254\timg = Image.open(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/photo.jpg\")\n   255\tprint(img.size, img.mode, img.format)\n   256\t```\n   257\t\n   258\tFor OCR on an image (text extraction, not description):\n   259\t\n   260\t```python\n   261\timport pytesseract\n   262\tprint(pytesseract.image_to_string(img))\n   263\t```\n   264\t\n   265\tNote: the client resizes images larger than 2000×2000 down to that\n   266\tbound and re-encodes as JPEG before upload, so the disk copy may not\n   267\tbe the user's original bytes. For most processing this doesn't matter;\n   268\tif the user is asking about original-resolution pixel data, flag it.\n   269\t\n   270\t---\n   271\t\n   272\t## Archives (ZIP / TAR / TAR.GZ)\n   273\t\n   274\t**List first. Extract never — unless the user explicitly asks.**\n   275\tArchives can be huge, contain path traversal, or nest forever.\n   276\t\n   277\t```bash\n   278\tunzip -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip\n   279\ttar -tf /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.tar\n   280\t```\n   281\t\n   282\tGNU tar auto-detects compression — `tar -tf` works on `.tar`,\n   283\t`.tar.gz`, `.tar.bz2`, `.tar.xz` alike. Don't hard-code `-z`.\n   284\t\n   285\tIf the user wants one file from inside, extract just that one:\n   286\t\n   287\t```bash\n   288\tunzip -p /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip path/inside/file.txt\n   289\t```\n   290\t\n   291\t**Standalone `.gz`** (not a tar) compresses a single file — there is\n   292\tno manifest to list. Just peek at the decompressed content:\n   293\t\n   294\t```bash\n   295\tzcat /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json.gz | head -50\n   296\t```\n   297\t\n   298\t---\n   299\t\n   300\t## EPUB / ODT\n   301\t\n   302\t```bash\n   303\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/book.epub | head -200\n   304\t```\n   305\t\n   306\tFor long ebooks, pipe through `head` — you rarely need the whole thing\n   307\tto answer a question.\n   308\t\n   309\t---\n   310\t\n   311\t## RTF / IPYNB\n   312\t\n   313\t```bash\n   314\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/notes.rtf | head -200\n   315\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/notebook.ipynb | head -200\n   316\t```\n   317\t\n   318\t---\n   319\t\n   320\t## Plain text / code / logs\n   321\t\n   322\tCheck the size first:\n   323\t\n   324\t```bash\n   325\twc -c /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log\n   326\t```\n   327\t\n   328\t- **Under ~20KB**: `cat` is fine.\n   329\t- **Over ~20KB**: `head -100` and `tail -100` to orient. If the user\n   330\t  asked about something specific, `grep` for it. Load the whole thing\n   331\t  only if you genuinely need all of it.\n   332\t\n   333\tFor log files, the user almost always cares about the end:\n   334\t\n   335\t```bash\n   336\ttail -200 /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log\n   337\t```\n   338\t\n   339\t---\n   340\t\n   341\t## Unknown extension\n   342\t\n   343\t```bash\n   344\tfile /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin\n   345\txxd /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin | head -5\n   346\t```\n   347\t\n   348\t`file` identifies most things. `xxd` head shows magic bytes. If `file`\n   349\tsays \"data\" and the hex doesn't match anything you recognize, ask the\n   350\tuser what it is instead of guessing.\n   351\t",

---\\nimport json\\r\\nimport math\\r\\nfrom collections import deque\\r\\n\\r\\ndef build_graph(layout):\\r\\n    graph = {}\\r\\n    for room in layout[\\\"rooms\\\"]:\\r\\n        graph[room[\\\"id\\\"]] = []\\r\\n    for door in layout[\\\"doors\\\"]:\\r\\n        if len(door[\\\"connects\\\"]) < 2:\\r\\n            continue\\r\\n        r1, r2 = door[\\\"connects\\\"]\\r\\n        graph[r1].append({\\\"room\\\": r2, \\\"door\\\": door[\\\"id\\\"], \\\"position\\\": door[\\\"position\\\"]})\\r\\n        graph[r2].append({\\\"room\\\": r1, \\\"door\\\": door[\\\"id\\\"], \\\"position\\\": door[\\\"position\\\"]})\\r\\n    return graph\\r\\n\\r\\ndef get_room_id(layout, name):\\r\\n    for room in layout[\\\"rooms\\\"]:\\r\\n        if room[\\\"name\\\"] == name:\\r\\n            return room[\\\"id\\\"]\\r\\n    return None\\r\\n\\r\\ndef get_room_name(layout, room_id):\\r\\n    for room in layout[\\\"rooms\\\"]:\\r\\n        if room[\\\"id\\\"] == room_id:\\r\\n            return room[\\\"name\\\"]\\r\\n    return room_id\\r\\n\\r\\ndef bfs_shortest_paths(graph, start_id):\\r\\n    distances = {start_id: 0}\\r\\n    doors_used = {start_id: []}\\r\\n    queue = deque([start_id])\\r\\n    while queue:\\r\\n        current = queue.popleft()\\r\\n        for neighbor in graph[current]:\\r\\n            nid = neighbor[\\\"room\\\"]\\r\\n            if nid not in distances:\\r\\n                distances[nid] = distances[current] + 1\\r\\n                doors_used[nid] = doors_used[current] + [neighbor[\\\"door\\\"]]\\r\\n                queue.append(nid)\\r\\n    return distances, doors_used\\r\\n\\r\\ndef door_position_score(layout, graph, start_id, distances):\\r\\n    door_scores = {}\\r\\n    for door in layout[\\\"doors\\\"]:\\r\\n        if len(door[\\\"connects\\\"]) < 2:\\r\\n            continue\\r\\n        r1, r2 = door[\\\"connects\\\"]\\r\\n        d1 = distances.get(r1, 999)\\r\\n        d2 = distances.get(r2, 999)\\r\\n        far_room = r2 if d1 < d2 else r1\\r\\n        near_room = r1 if d1 < d2 else r2\\r\\n        alt_connections = [n for n in graph[near_room] if n[\\\"room\\\"] != far_room]\\r\\n        if alt_connections:\\r\\n            avg_x = sum(n[\\\"position\\\"][0] for n in alt_connections) / len(alt_connections)\\r\\n            avg_y = sum(n[\\\"position\\\"][1] for n in alt_connections) / len(alt_connections)\\r\\n            dx = door[\\\"position\\\"][0] - avg_x\\r\\n            dy = door[\\\"position\\\"][1] - avg_y\\r\\n            deviation = math.sqrt(dx**2 + dy**2)\\r\\n        else:\\r\\n            deviation = 0\\r\\n        if deviation < 1.0:\\r\\n            score = 1\\r\\n            label = \\\"optimal\\\"\\r\\n            advice = \\\"Door position is well placed.\\\"\\r\\n        elif deviation < 2.5:\\r\\n            score = 2\\r\\n            label = \\\"minor issue\\\"\\r\\n            advice = \\\"Consider shifting {} closer to the main circulation center.\\\".format(door[\\\"id\\\"])\\r\\n        else:\\r\\n            score = 3\\r\\n            label = \\\"bottleneck\\\"\\r\\n            advice = \\\"Door {} is far from the main path - shifting it could shorten access.\\\".format(door[\\\"id\\\"])\\r\\n        door_scores[door[\\\"id\\\"]] = {\\r\\n            \\\"door_id\\\": door[\\\"id\\\"],\\r\\n            \\\"connects\\\": door[\\\"connects\\\"],\\r\\n            \\\"position\\\": door[\\\"position\\\"],\\r\\n            \\\"deviation\\\": round(deviation, 2),\\r\\n            \\\"score\\\": score,\\r\\n            \\\"label\\\": label,\\r\\n            \\\"advice\\\": advice\\r\\n        }\\r\\n    return doo\\n\\n--- Script 15 ---\\nimport json\\r\\n\\r\\ntry:\\r\\n    # 1) layout _coerce_to_string(value):\\r\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Accept str, list of str, or anything stringifiable; join lists with newlines.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\r\\n    if value is None:\\r\\n        return None\\r\\n    if isinstance(value, str):\\r\\n        return value\\r\\n    try:\\r\\n        return \\\"\\\\n\\\".join(str(v) for v in value)\\r\\n    except TypeError:\\r\\n        return deque([start_id])\\r\\n    while queue:\\r\\n        current = queue.popleft()\\r\\n        for neighbor in graph[current]:\\r\\n            nid = neighbor[\\\"room\\\"]\\r\\n            if nid not in distances:\\r\\n                distances[nid] = distances[current] + 1\\r\\n                doors_used[nid] = doors_used[current] + [neighbor[\\\"door\\\"]]\\r\\n                queue.append(nid)\\r\\n    return distances, doors_used\\r\\n\\r\\ndef door_position_score(layout, graph, start_id, distances):\\r\\n    door_scores = {}\\r\\n    for door in = distances.get(r2, 999)\\r\\n        far_room = r2 if d1 < d2 else r1\\r\\n        near_room = r1 if d1 < d2 else r2\\r\\n        alt_connections = [n for n in graph[near_room] if n[\\\"room\\\"] != far_room]\\r\\n        if alt_connections:\\r\\n            avg_x = sum(n[\\\"position\\\"][0] for n in alt_connections) / len(alt_connections)\\r\\n            avg_y = sum(n[\\\"position\\\"][1] for n in alt_connections) / len(alt_connections)\\r\\n            dx = door[\\\"position\\\"][0] - avg_x\\r\\n            dy = door[\\\"position\\\"][1] - avg_y\\r\\n            deviation = math.sqrt(dx**2 + dy**2)\\r\\n        else:\\r\\n            deviation = 0\\r\\n        if deviation < 1.0:\\r\\n            score = 1\\r\\n            label = \\\"optimal\\\"\\r\\n            advice = \\\"Door position is well placed.\\\"\\r\\n        elif deviation < 2.5:\\r\\n            score = 2\\r\\n            label = \\\"minor issue\\\"\\r\\n            advice = \\\"Consider shifting {} closer to the main circulation center.\\\".format(door[\\\"id\\\"])\\r\\n        else:\\r\\n            score = 3\\r\\n            label = \\\"bottleneck\\\"\\r\\n            advice = \\\"Door {} is far from the main path - shifting it could shorten access.\\\".format(door[\\\"id\\\"])\\r\\n        door_scores[door[\\\"id\\\"]] = {\\r\\n            \\\"door_id\\\": door[\\\"id\\\"],\\r\\n            \\\"connects\\\": door[\\\"connects\\\"],\\r\\n            \\\"position\\\": door[\\\"position\\\"],\\r\\n            \\\"deviation\\\": round(deviation, 2),\\r\\n            \\\"score\\\": score,\\r\\n            \\\"label\\\": label,\\r\\n            \\\"advice\\\": advice\\r\\n        }\\r\\n    return doo\\n\\n--- Script 15 ---\\nimport json\\r\\n\\r\\ntry:\\r\\n    # 1) layout         {
            "uuid": "019e31ba-266b-7dbf-8805-8fa9b373c382",
            "text": "",
            "content": [
                {
                    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-16T16:59:06.043331Z",
                    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-16T16:59:14.795001Z",
                    "flags": null,
                    "type": "text",
                    "text": " **The core question:** From a point in space, what can you see?\n\n---\n\n**Step 1 — Define the source point**\nPick the center of the source room. This is where the observer stands (or sits).\n\n---\n\n**Step 2 — Sample target points**\nFill every other room with a grid of sample points — one point every X meters. The denser the grid, the more accurate the result.\n\n---\n\n**Step 3 — Cast a ray**\nFor each sample point, draw a straight line from the source center to that point. This is the sightline.\n\n---\n\n**Step 4 — Check for obstruction**\nDoes that line cross any wall? If yes → blocked. If no → visible.\n\n---\n\n**Step 5 — Calculate the score**\nFor each room:\n```\nscore = visible points / total points × 100\n```\nSo if 7 out of 10 sample points are visible → 70% visibility.\n\n---\n\n**Step 6 — Run at 2 heights**\nDo the same calculation twice:\n- At **0.9m** → seated eye level\n- At **1.6m** → standing eye level\n\nAn object might be visible standing but blocked when seated. Both scores matter.\n\n---\n\n**What it outputs:**\n```json\n{\n  \"seated_0.9m\": {\n    \"Compressor Room\": 45.0,\n    \"Rest Area\": 80.0,\n    \"Board Room\": 20.0\n  },\n  \"standing_1.6m\": {\n    \"Compressor Room\": 60.0,\n    \"Rest Area\": 90.0,\n    \"Board Room\": 35.0\n  },\n  \"best_visible\": \"Rest Area\",\n  \"worst_visible\": \"Board Room\"\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n**The key library — Shapely:**\n- Room polygons → `Polygon`\n- Walls → `LineString`\n- Sightline → `LineString`\n- Intersection check → `sightline.intersects(wall)`\n\nSimple, fast, no Rhino needed.\n\n---\n\nDoes this make sense before Claude Code writes it?",
                    "citations": []
                }
            ],
            "sender": "assistant",
            "index": 157,
            "created_at": "2026-05-16T16:59:14.833366Z",
            "updated_at": "2026-05-16T16:59:14.833366Z",
            "input_mode": null,
            "truncated": false,
            "stop_reason": "stop_sequence",
            "compaction_summary": null,

                    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-16T17:08:20.474582Z",
                    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-16T17:08:20.474582Z",
                    "flags": null,
                    "type": "text",
                    "text": "\"\"\"\nnodes/visibility.py — Line-of-sight analysis between rooms (Mode 1) and objects (Mode 2).\nMode 1 (no objects): centroid of source room → centroid of target room\nMode 2 (objects placed): object use_point → object functional_point\nHeights 0.9m (seated) and 1.6m (standing) are tracked.\nIn Mode 1 both heights give the same 2D result — no height-varying elements exist yet.\nMode 2 will differentiate them when object heights are known.\nRequires: shapely\n\"\"\"\nfrom __future__ import annotations\nfrom typing import Any\nfrom shapely.geometry import LineString, Polygon\nfrom shapely.ops import unary_union\nSEATED_HEIGHT = 0.9\nSTANDING_HEIGHT = 1.6\ndef _make_polygon(pts: list) -> Polygon:\n    return Polygon(pts)\ndef _centroid(pts: list) -> tuple[float, float]:\n    p = _make_polygon(pts)\n    return (p.centroid.x, p.centroid.y)\ndef _build_interior(rooms: list[dict]) -> Any:\n    \"\"\"Union of all room polygons — the traversable floor area.\"\"\"\n    polys = [_make_polygon(r[\"geometry\"]) for r in rooms if r.get(\"geometry\")]\n    return unary_union(polys) if polys else None\ndef _has_objects(layout: dict) -> bool:\n    return bool(layout.get(\"objects\") or layout.get(\"furniture\"))\ndef _find_blocker(ray: LineString, rooms: list[dict]) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Return the name/id of the first room boundary the ray crosses.\"\"\"\n    for room in rooms:\n        geom = room.get(\"geometry\")\n        if not geom:\n            continue\n        if ray.crosses(_make_polygon(geom).exterior):\n            return room.get(\"name\") or room.get(\"id\") or \"wall\"\n    return \"wall\"\ndef _check_sightline(\n    src: tuple[float, float],\n    tgt: tuple[float, float],\n    interior: Any,\n    rooms: list[dict],\n    src_label: str,\n    tgt_label: str,\n) -> dict[str, Any]:\n    ray = LineString([src, tgt])\n    # interior.covers() allows boundary contact (doors/shared edges pass through)\n    blocked_by = None if interior.covers(ray) else _find_blocker(ray, rooms)\n    return {\n        \"source\": src_label,\n        \"target\": tgt_label,\n        \"visible_seated\": blocked_by is None,\n        \"visible_standing\": blocked_by is None,\n        \"blocked_by\": blocked_by,\n    }\ndef check_visibility(layout: dict[str, Any]) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Check line-of-sight for all pairs in the layout.\n    Auto-detects mode: room centroids (Mode 1) or object points (Mode 2).\n    Returns one result dict per pair.\n    \"\"\"\n    rooms = layout.get(\"rooms\", [])\n    interior = _build_interior(rooms)\n    if interior is None:\n        return []\n    results = []\n    if _has_objects(layout):\n        # Mode 2 — object use_point → functional_point\n        objects = layout.get(\"objects\") or layout.get(\"furniture\") or []\n        for i, src_obj in enumerate(objects):\n            for tgt_obj in objects[i + 1:]:\n                src_pt = src_obj.get(\"use_point\") or src_obj.get(\"position\")\n                tgt_pt = tgt_obj.get(\"functional_point\") or tgt_obj.get(\"position\")\n                if not src_pt or not tgt_pt:\n                    continue\n                results.append(_check_sightline(\n                    tuple(src_pt[:2]), tuple(tgt_pt[:2]),\n                    interior, rooms,\n                    src_obj.get(\"id\") or src_obj.get(\"name\") or \"obj\",\n                    tgt_obj.get(\"id\") or tgt_obj.get(\"name\") or \"obj\",\n                ))\n    else:\n        # Mode 1 — room centroid → room centroid\n        valid = [r for r in rooms if r.get(\"geometry\")]\n        for i, src_room in enumerate(valid):\n            for tgt_room in valid[i + 1:]:\n                results.append(_check_sightline(\n                    _centroid(src_room[\"geometry\"]),\n                    _centroid(tgt_room[\"geometry\"]),\n                    interior, rooms,\n                    src_room.get(\"name\") or src_room.get(\"id\") or \"room\",\n                    tgt_room.get(\"name\") or tgt_room.get(\"id\") or \"room\",\n                ))\n    return results\n\n\nThree design decisions worth noting:\n\n1. `interior.covers(ray)` not `interior.contains(ray)` — `covers` allows the ray to touch boundary edges (shared walls, door thresholds), which would otherwise falsely report blocking on adjacent rooms.\n2. `_find_blocker` uses `ray.crosses(boundary)` not `ray.intersects` — `crosses` requires the ray to actually pass through the boundary (not just touch it at a point), avoiding false positives at shared corners.\n3. Both heights return the same result in Mode 1 — there are no height-varying elements in the 2D layout. The seated/standing split is structural so Mode 2 can differentiate them when object heights are present.\nSay the word and I'll write the file.",

                    "type": "text",
                    "text": "Minimal fix — two changes:\n`_find_blocker` gains `src_label` and `tgt_label` params, skips segments belonging to either:\n\n```python\ndef _find_blocker(\n    ray: LineString,\n    rooms: list[dict],\n    doors: list[dict],\n    src_label: str,\n    tgt_label: str,\n) -> str | None:\n    \"\"\"Return label of the first wall the ray crosses that is not the source/target room or a door.\"\"\"\n    for seg, label in _wall_segments(rooms):\n        if label == src_label or label == tgt_label:   # ← skip own walls\n            continue\n        if not ray.crosses(seg):\n            continue\n        cross = ray.intersection(seg)\n        if cross.is_empty:\n            continue\n        cross_pt = (\n            Point(cross.x, cross.y)\n            if hasattr(cross, \"x\")\n            else Point(*list(cross.geoms)[0].coords[0])\n        )\n        if not _near_door(cross_pt, doors):\n            return label\n    return None\n\n```\n\n`_check_sightline` forwards them:\n\n```python\ndef _check_sightline(\n    src: tuple[float, float],\n    tgt: tuple[float, float],\n    rooms: list[dict],\n    doors: list[dict],\n    src_label: str,\n    tgt_label: str,\n) -> dict[str, Any]:\n    ray = LineString([src, tgt])\n    blocked_by = _find_blocker(ray, rooms, doors, src_label, tgt_label)   # ← pass labels\n    return {\n        \"source\": src_label,\n        \"target\": tgt_label,\n        \"visible_seated\": blocked_by is None,\n        \"visible_standing\": blocked_by is None,\n        \"blocked_by\": blocked_by,\n    }\n\n```\n\nEverything else stays the same.                     "text": " When objects are placed, the isovist logic shifts from **room-to-room** to **object-to-object** and **object-to-space**.\n\n---\n\n**The logic becomes:**\n\n**From the object's use point** (where the person stands):\n- Cast rays in all directions\n- Find what's visible from that position\n- Draw the isovist polygon showing the visible area\n\n---\n\n**Practical examples:**\n\n**TV + Sofa:**\n- Isovist from sofa use point → does it reach the TV screen?\n- Isovist from TV → how much of the seating area is covered?\n\n**Factory machine + Operator:**\n- Isovist from operator station → can they see all machines they need to monitor?\n- Blocked areas flagged immediately\n\n**Restaurant kitchen:**\n- Isovist from each workstation → do workflows overlap or stay clear?\n\n---\n\n**So the visibility tool has 3 stages:**\n\n```\nStage 1 — Empty room (now)\nRoom centroid → Room centroid\nSimple ray, no isovist needed\n\nStage 2 — Objects placed (Mode 2)\nObject use point → full isovist\nShows visible area from that position\n\nStage 3 — Multiple objects\nEach object gets its own isovist\nOverlapping isovists = shared visibility zones\nAgent reasons about whether overlap is good or bad\ndepending on space type\n```\n\n---\n\n**For now** — build the Stage 1 visualization (simple lines or basic isovist from room centers). Stage 2 activates automatically when objects exist in the layout — that's the Mode 2 switch already in your visibility tool.\n\nDo you want to go with the isovist visualization or the simple lines for Stage 1?",

                    "text": "```python\nfrom __future__ import annotations\nimport json\nfrom pathlib import Path\nfrom typing import Any\nfrom _runtime.llm import write_tool_result\n\n\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n# Tool node — executes MCP tool calls requested by the reason node.\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\ndef build_tool_node(mcp_client, allowed_tools, edited_layout_path):\n    \"\"\"Return a tool node function ready to be added to a LangGraph StateGraph.\"\"\"\n\n    allowed_names = {t[\"name\"] for t in allowed_tools if t.get(\"name\")}\n\n    def tool_node(state):\n\n        # Iterate over the pending tool calls\n        for call in state[\"pending_tool_calls\"]:\n\n            # Stop the process if max number of iterations is reached\n            state[\"iteration\"] += 1\n            if state[\"iteration\"] > state[\"max_iterations\"]:\n                raise RuntimeError(\"Max iterations exceeded\")\n\n\n            # Get the tool name and check its valid\n            tool_name = call[\"name\"]\n            if tool_name not in allowed_names:\n                raise RuntimeError(f\"Tool '{tool_name}' is not in the allowed tools list\")\n            \n            print(f\"Calling tool: {tool_name} with arguments: {call['arguments']}\")\n\n            # Cleanup any null values accidentally included by the LLM\n            tool_args = {k: v for k, v in call[\"arguments\"].items() if v is not None}\n\n            # Inject layout_json\n            if \"layout_json\" in tool_args:\n                tool_args[\"layout_json\"] = state[\"layout_json_string\"]\n\n            # Call the tool\n            tool_output = mcp_client.call_tool(tool_name, tool_args)\n\n            # Store the updated layout returned by the MCP tool to a json file\n            write_tool_result(tool_output, edited_layout_path)\n\n            # If the tool returned valid JSON, update the layout in state so\n            # subsequent tool calls in this loop receive the latest layout.\n            try:\n                updated = json.loads(tool_output.strip())\n                if isinstance(updated, dict):\n                    state[\"layout_json_string\"] = json.dumps(updated)\n            except (json.JSONDecodeError, AttributeError):\n                pass\n\n            # Append the tool call and its result to the conversation history\n            state[\"messages\"].append({\n                \"role\": \"assistant\",\n                \"content\": json.dumps({\n                    \"action\": \"tool\",\n                    \"final_response\": \"\",\n                    \"tool_calls\": [{\"name\": tool_name, \"arguments\": tool_args}],\n                }),\n            })\n            \n            state[\"messages\"].append({\n                \"role\": \"user\",\n                \"content\": f\"Tool result: {tool_output}\",\n            })\n            print(f\"Tool result: {tool_output}\")\n\n        state[\"pending_tool_calls\"] = None\n        return state\n\n    return tool_node\n\n```\n\nthis is the tool.py file example giving by scott and joao can you expalin it ?",

                    "text": " This is the existing `tools.py` node — the wrapper that sits in the graph and executes MCP tool calls. Let me break it down:\n\n---\n\n**`build_tool_node(mcp_client, allowed_tools, edited_layout_path)`**\nA factory function — it builds and returns the tool node. Takes 3 things:\n- `mcp_client` → the connection to GH via Swiftlet\n- `allowed_tools` → whitelist of valid tool names\n- `edited_layout_path` → where to save the updated layout JSON\n\n---\n\n**Inside `tool_node(state)`:**\n\n**1 — Iteration guard**\n```python\nstate[\"iteration\"] += 1\nif state[\"iteration\"] > state[\"max_iterations\"]:\n    raise RuntimeError(\"Max iterations exceeded\")\n```\nPrevents infinite loops — if the agent keeps calling tools without finishing, it stops.\n\n---\n\n**2 — Tool validation**\n```python\nif tool_name not in allowed_names:\n    raise RuntimeError(...)\n```\nOnly tools in the whitelist can be called. Safety check.\n\n---\n\n**3 — Layout injection**\n```python\nif \"layout_json\" in tool_args:\n    tool_args[\"layout_json\"] = state[\"layout_json_string\"]\n```\nEvery tool that needs the layout gets the **current state** of the layout — not a stale version. Critical for the loop logic.\n\n---\n\n**4 — MCP call**\n```python\ntool_output = mcp_client.call_tool(tool_name, tool_args)\n```\nActually calls the GH tool via MCP and gets the result back.\n\n---\n\n**5 — Layout state update**\n```python\nupdated = json.loads(tool_output.strip())\nif isinstance(updated, dict):\n    state[\"layout_json_string\"] = json.dumps(updated)\n```\nIf the tool returned an updated layout JSON, it immediately updates the state. So the next tool call in the same loop gets the fresh layout. This is your **evaluate → modify → re-evaluate** loop working in practice.\n\n---\n\n**6 — Conversation history**\n```python\nstate[\"messages\"].append({...})\n```\nAppends both the tool call and its result to the message history. The LLM sees this in the next reason step and knows what happened.\n\n---\n\n**In short:**\nThis node is the **executor**. The reason node decides what to call, this node calls it, updates the state, and hands back to the graph. It's the bridge between LLM reasoning and GH action.\n\n---\n\n**What needs to change for your architecture:**\nCurrently `allowed_names` is hardcoded to 5 GH tools. As you add new tools — including `visualize_visibility` — they need to be added to the whitelist. Eventually this should be dynamic, reading from whatever tools MCP discovers at startup.\n\nMakes sense?",

3) Inyectar layout_json solo si el tool lo necesita\n            if tool_name in TOOLS_NEEDING_LAYOUT:\n                tool_args[\"layout_json\"] = state[\"layout_json_string\"]\n\n            print(f\"Calling tool: {tool_name} with arguments (cleaned): {list(tool_args.keys())}\")\n\n            # 6) Si vino layout actualizado, refrescar state\n            try:\n                updated = json.loads(tool_output.strip())\n                if isinstance(updated, dict):\n                    if \"rooms\" in updated:\n                        state[\"layout_json_string\"] = json.dumps(updated)\n                    elif \"doors\" in updated:\n                        # 7) Anadir a historial\n            state[\"messages\"].append({\n                \"role\": \"assistant\",\n                \"content\": json.dumps({\n                    \"action\": \"tool\",\n                    \"final_response\": \"\",\n                    \"tool_calls\": [{\"name\": tool_name, \"arguments\": {k: v for k, v in tool_args.items() if k != \"layout_json\"}}],\n                }),\n            })\n\n            state[\"messages\"].append({\n                \"role\": \"user\",\n                \"content\": f\"Tool result: {tool_output}\",\n            })\n\n            print(f\"Tool result: {tool_output[:500]}\")  # Recortar log para no saturar terminal\n\n        state[\"pending_tool_calls\"] = None\n        return state\n\n    return tool_node\n```\n\nmy friends while working they adjusted the tools.py but i have a backup of the clean code that i already shared with you",

Core mechanic: place an object → everything changes (visibility, collision, path, reachability). Agent places objects, runs parallel analysis, evaluates, adjusts, and delivers best configuration with reasoning.\n\n## Architecture (State Graph)\n[DECISION] Full graph designed and visualized with parallel tool groups:\n\n**Pre-agent layer (parallel, optional):**\n- Space Type Agent → space rules + priorities + tool weights\n- Profile Agent → user needs + constraints (mobility, reach)\nBoth feed → LLM reason node\n\n**Main loop:**\n- LLM reason → Add Object (GH via MCP)\n- Group 1 parallel: Collision + Visibility + Orientation (optional)\n- LLM evaluate → bad: Adjust+retry / good: continue\n- Group 2 parallel: Path analysis + Reachability\n- LLM evaluate → bad: Adjust+retry / good: continue\n- More objects? yes→loop / no→User checkpoint\n- Approved → Score layout → LLM explain → Output to GH\n\n**Satellite:** Query Agent (independent, callable anytime)\n\n## File Structure\n```\nteam_03/\n├── layout/\n│   ├── industrial_100/   (industrial_002.json, industrial_003.json, industrial_005.json, etc.)\n│   └── residential_100/  (residential_01.json, etc.)\n├── workspace/            ← live session file (session_active.json)\n├── output/               ← final approved layouts (timestamped)\n├── knowledge/            ← RAG knowledge base\n│   ├── __init__.py\n│   ├── loader.py\n│   ├── general/          (accessibility_codes.json, spatial_ergonomics.json)\n│   ├── industrial/       (machinery_spacing.json, osha_guidelines.json)\n│   └── residential/      (ada_clearances.json, furniture_clearances.json)\n└── python/\n    ├── _runtime/\n    │   ├── bootstrap.py  ✓ UPDATED\n    │   ├── config.py     ✓ UPDATED\n    │   ├── llm.py        ✓ UPDATED (multi-provider + call_llm_simple)\n    │   ├── mcp_client.py\n    │   └── session.py    ✓ NEW\n    ├── nodes/\n    │   ├── tools.py      ✓ UPDATED (dynamic schema injection, workspace_path closure)\n    │   ├── visibility.py ✓ COMPLETE\n    │   ├── path_analysis.py ✓ COMPLETE\n    │   ├── reachability.py ✓ COMPLETE\n    │   ├── orientation.py  ✓ COMPLETE\n    │   ├── add_objects.py  ✓ NEW\n    │   ├── reason.py       ✓ UPDATED (teacher's r03 version)\n    │   ├── profile_agent.py  ✓ FROM RAMON (verified good)\n    │   └── space_type_agent.py ✓ FROM RAMON (verified good)\n    ├── graph.py           ← OLD, needs Phase 3 rewrite\n    ├── main.py            ← untouched\n    └── test_bootstrap.py  ✓ UPDATED (all tool tests)\n```\n\n## Completed Files\n\n### _runtime/session.py (NEW)\nFour functions: `create_session`, `save_session`, `close_session`, `detect_existing_session`.                     "type": "text",
                    "text": " **Collision Detection — The Core Question:**\nDoes this object overlap with anything it shouldn't?\n\n---\n\n**What it checks:**\n\n**1 — Object vs Object**\nDo any two furniture/object polygons intersect each other? A sofa and a table can't occupy the same space.\n\n**2 — Object vs Wall**\nDoes any object overlap with a wall boundary? A sofa can't be placed inside a wall.\n\n**3 — Object vs Door**\nDoes any object block a door opening? A wardrobe placed in front of a door is a collision even if it doesn't touch the wall.\n\n---\n\n**How it works:**\n\n**Step 1 — Build polygons**\nConvert every object's geometry into a Shapely polygon. Same for room boundaries and door openings.\n\n**Step 2 — Check pairs**\nFor every object, check it against:\n- Every other object → do they intersect?\n- Every room wall boundary → does it overlap?\n- Every door → does it block the opening?\n\n**Step 3 — Optional clearance**\nIf `use_clearance=True`, inflate each object polygon by the clearance value before checking. So a machine needing 1.2m clearance gets its polygon expanded by 1.2m — if the expanded version collides, it's flagged even if the object itself doesn't touch anything.\n\n**Step 4 — Output**\n```json\n{\n  \"object_a\": \"furn-sofa-01\",\n  \"object_b\": \"furn-table-01\",\n  \"collision\": true,\n  \"clearance_violation\": false,\n  \"overlap_area\": 0.45\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n**What Shapely gives you:**\n- `polygon_a.intersects(polygon_b)` → true/false\n- `polygon_a.intersection(polygon_b).area` → how much overlap\n- `polygon_a.buffer(1.2)` → inflate by clearance value\n\n---\n\n**Mode detection — same as visibility:**\n- No objects in layout → check room boundaries against each other (are rooms overlapping? shouldn't happen but catches bad JSON)\n- Objects present → check objects against each other and against walls/doors\n\n---\n\n**The clearance toggle:**\n```python\ndef check_collision(layout, use_clearance=False, clearance_value=0.0):\n```\nUser sets it via Space Type Agent at the start. Factory floor → clearance on, strict values. Quick sketch → clearance off.\n\n---\n\nDoes this make sense before building it?",

                    "flags": null,
                    "type": "text",
                    "text": " Good — this one is 80% already written from the GH scripts. Here's the logic first:\n\n---\n\n**The core question:** Can you get from point A to point B, and how efficiently?\n\n---\n\n**How it works:**\n\n**Step 1 — Build a room graph**\nConvert the layout into a graph where:\n- Each room = a node\n- Each door = an edge connecting two rooms\n- Edge weight = distance between room centroids\n\n**Step 2 — Shortest path (A\\* or BFS)**\nFind the most efficient route from start room to end room. Returns the sequence of rooms and doors traversed.\n\n**Step 3 — Longest path**\nFind the two rooms that are farthest apart in the graph. This is the worst-case egress scenario — the most isolated point in the layout.\n\n**Step 4 — All pairs**\nRun shortest path for every room pair. This gives a complete picture of how connected the layout is.\n\n---\n\n**What it outputs:**\n```json\n{\n  \"start\": \"Receiving Area\",\n  \"end\": \"Distribution Floor\",\n  \"path\": [\"Receiving Area\", \"Distribution Floor\"],\n  \"doors_used\": [\"door-01\"],\n  \"steps\": 1,\n  \"distance\": 12.5,\n  \"status\": \"reachable\",\n  \"longest_path\": {\n    \"from\": \"Utility Room\",\n    \"to\": \"Receiving Area\",\n    \"steps\": 4,\n    \"distance\": 45.2\n  }\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n**Tell Claude Code:**\n\n```\nCreate nodes/path_analysis.py\n\nPort the BFS logic from the GH scripts into \nclean Python. \n\nFunction: check_path(layout, start_room, end_room)\n\nStep 1: Build graph from rooms and doors\n  - rooms = nodes\n  - doors = edges (use \"connects\" or \n    \"connectsRooms\" from door attributes)\n  - edge weight = euclidean distance \n    between room centroids\n\nStep 2: BFS shortest path from start to end\n  - returns path (room name list)\n  - doors used (door id list)  \n  - number of steps\n  - total distance\n  - status: reachable / unreachable\n\nStep 3: Longest path\n  - run BFS from every room\n  - find the two rooms with maximum distance\n  - return as worst case egress\n\nStep 4: Also run build_path_node(mcp_client)\n  following same pattern as visibility.py\n  - calls check_path on layout\n  - calls mcp_client visualize_path (for later)\n  - stores results in state\n\nNo Rhino. Pure Python only.\nUse collections.deque for BFS.\nShow code before writing.\n```",

                    "type": "text",
                    "text": " Two really important observations. Let me address both:\n\n---\n\n**1 — Non-rectangular rooms (L-shape, T-shape)**\n\nYou're right — the centroid of an L-shaped polygon falls outside the room boundary. A ray or path starting from that point would be invalid.\n\n**The fix — use the largest inscribed point instead of centroid:**\n\nThree options:\n\n**Option A — Pole of inaccessibility**\nThe point inside the polygon that is farthest from any boundary. Always guaranteed to be inside. Shapely has this via `polylabel` algorithm.\n\n**Option B — Snap centroid to interior**\nCalculate centroid, if it's outside the polygon find the nearest point on the polygon that IS inside. Quick but less accurate.\n\n**Option C — Skeleton point**\nFind the medial axis of the room and use its center. More complex but gives the most spatially meaningful point.\n\n**Recommendation: Option A** — polylabel. One line in Shapely, always inside, geometrically meaningful.\n\n---\n\n**2 — Path analysis with objects vs rooms**\n\nThis is the bigger question and you're right to flag it. Currently path analysis works at **room level** — navigating between rooms through doors.\n\nBut your concept is about **object placement** — can a worker get from the CNC machine to the storage unit efficiently? That's a different scale.\n\n**The two levels:**\n\n```\nRoom level (current):\nReceiving Area → Distribution Floor → Shipping Area\nNavigates through doors between rooms\nGood for: overall layout analysis, egress\n\nObject level (needed for your concept):\nCNC Machine → Operator Station → Material Storage\nNavigates around objects within a room\nGood for: furniture/equipment placement optimization\n```\n\n**For object-level path analysis you need:**\n- A 2D grid of the room (not the whole building)\n- Objects placed as obstacles on that grid\n- A* pathfinding around those obstacles\n- This is the bookmarked path analysis tool in Python\n\n---\n\n**So path analysis splits into two tools:**\n\n```\nnodes/path_analysis_rooms.py  \n→ BFS between rooms via doors\n→ works now with existing layout\n→ good for egress, overall connectivity\n\nnodes/path_analysis_objects.py  \n→ A* grid-based within a room\n→ works when objects are placed\n→ good for workflow, furniture optimization\n```\n\n---\n\n**Which do you want to build first?**",

                    "type": "text",
                    "text": " No — path analysis and reachability are different things:\n\n---\n\n**Path analysis** — can you get from A to B and how?\n- Finds the route\n- Measures distance and steps\n- Identifies if a destination is reachable at all\n- Works at room level or object level\n\n---\n\n**Reachability** — once you're at the object, can your body physically access it?\n- Checks reach radius (how far your arm extends)\n- Checks reach height (min and max you can reach)\n- Depends on user profile (wheelchair vs standing)\n- Has nothing to do with navigation — you're already there\n\n---\n\n**Simple analogy:**\n- Path analysis = can you walk to the fridge?\n- Reachability = once at the fridge, can you open the top shelf?\n\n---\n\n**They work together in the graph:**\n\n```\nPath analysis runs first\n→ can the operator GET to the machine? ✓\n        ↓\nReachability runs after\n→ once there, can they REACH the controls? ✓\n```\n\nTwo separate tools, two separate checks, both needed. Does this clarify it?",

Build centroid lookup for rooms and furniture\n    centroids = {}\n    for room in layout.get(\"rooms\", []):\n        name = room.get(\"name\")\n        pts = room.get(\"geometry\")\n        if name and pts:\n            xs = [p[0] for p in pts]\n            ys = [p[1] for p in pts]\n            centroids[name] = rg.Point3d(\n                sum(xs)/len(xs), sum(ys)/len(ys), 0)\n\n    for furn in layout.get(\"furniture\", []):\n        fid = furn.get(\"id\")\n        pts = furn.get(\"geometry\")\n        if fid and pts:\n            xs = [p[0] for p in pts]\n            ys = [p[1] for p in pts]\n            centroids[fid] = rg.Point3d(\n                sum(xs)/len(xs), sum(ys)/len(ys), 0)\n\n    reachable_lines = []\n    unreachable_lines = []\n\n    for pair in results.get(\"pairs\", []):\n        src = centroids.get(pair[\"source\"])\n        tgt = centroids.get(pair[\"target\"])\n        if not src or not tgt:\n            continue\n        line = rg.LineCurve(src, tgt)\n        if pair[\"status\"] == \"reachable\":\n            reachable_lines.append(line)\n        else:\n            unreachable_lines.append(line)\n\n    a = reachable_lines    # green\n    b = unreachable_lines  # red\n    out = f\"Drawn: {len(reachable_lines)} reachable, {len(unreachable_lines)} unreachable\"\n```\n\n- Output `a` → green Custom Preview\n- Output `b` → red Custom Preview\n\n---\n\nOnce done run:\n\n```powershell\npython test_bootstrap.py --layout industrial_03\n```",

Provide a layout name, e.g. --layout layout_schema\")\n    matches = list((team_dir / \"layout\").rglob(f\"{_known.layout}.json\"))\n    if not matches:\n        raise FileNotFoundError(f\"Layout '{_known.layout}.json' not found anywhere under layout/\")\n    resolved_layout = matches[0]\n    layout_data: dict[str, Any] = json.loads(resolved_layout.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\"))\n\n    # Connect to the Grasshopper MCP server and list available tools\n    mcp_client = McpClient(settings.mcp_endpoint, settings.request_timeout_seconds)\n    mcp_client.initialize()\n    tools = mcp_client.list_tools()\n    print(f\"Discovered MCP tools: {[t.get('name') for t in tools]}\")\n\n    # Build the LLM with a structured-output schema tailored to the available tools\n    llm = create_chat_llm(\n        api_key=settings.api_key,\n        base_url=settings.base_url,\n        llm_model=settings.llm_model,\n        timeout_seconds=settings.request_timeout_seconds,\n        model_kwargs=get_llm_response_format(tools),\n    )\n\n    team_name = team_dir.name\n    edited_layout_path = team_dir / f\"{team_name}_edited_layout.json\"\n\n    return Context(\n        llm=llm,\n        mcp_client=mcp_client,\n        tools=tools,\n        layout_data=layout_data,\n        max_iterations=settings.max_iterations,\n        edited_layout_path=edited_layout_path,\n    )\n```",

                    "type": "text",
                    "text": " Good question. Let me be precise about what it's actually computing right now:\n\n---\n\n**What it does:**\n\nIt finds the **shortest walkable route** between two objects (or rooms) by navigating around furniture obstacles on a grid.\n\n---\n\n**Mode 2 (object level) — what's actually happening:**\n\n1. Takes the room polygon and converts it to a grid of cells (0.5m each)\n2. Marks every furniture object as an obstacle on the grid — except the two objects being checked\n3. Runs A* from the centroid of object A to the centroid of object B\n4. A* finds the path that avoids all furniture in between\n5. Returns the distance in metres and number of steps\n\n---\n\n**What the results mean for `industrial_005`:**\n\n```\nfurn-3 → furn-4 | steps: 51 | distance: 25.0m\n```\nAn operator needs to walk 25 metres navigating around all other equipment to get from Assembly Station 1 to Packaging Station 2.\n\n```\nfurn-3 → furn-6 | steps: 7 | distance: 3.207m\n```\nOnly 3.2 metres between Assembly Station 1 and Packaging Station 4 — they're close neighbors.\n\n```\nfurn-6 → furn-12 | distance: 34.864m\n```\nWorst case — Packaging Station 4 to Conveyor Section 10 is the longest walk in the layout.\n\n---\n\n**What it's NOT doing yet:**\n- It's not checking if the path is wide enough for a person or wheelchair\n- It's not checking if the path crosses dangerous zones\n- It's not suggesting a better object placement\n\nThose come from reachability, collision, and the LLM reasoning layer.\n\n---\n\n**In one sentence:** Path analysis tells you how far and how efficiently a person can physically travel between any two objects in the space, navigating around everything in between.",

                    "type": "text",
                    "text": " **Orientation — What it is:**\n\nIt checks if an object is **facing the right direction** relative to another object or to a defined target.\n\nA sofa placed correctly in a room but facing the wall instead of the TV is functionally wrong even if collision passes and visibility passes. Orientation catches this.\n\n---\n\n**How it works:**\n\nEvery object has a **facing direction** — a vector pointing where its \"front\" is. For a sofa that's where the person sits and looks. For a machine that's where the operator stands.\n\nThe tool checks:\n- What direction is this object facing?\n- Is it facing toward its intended target?\n- Is the angle between facing direction and target within an acceptable range?\n\n---\n\n**Example:**\n\n```\nSofa facing vector: [0, 1] (facing north)\nTV position: [10, 15] (to the north)\nAngle between them: 5° → acceptable ✓\n\nSofa facing vector: [0, -1] (facing south)\nTV position: [10, 15] (to the north)\nAngle between them: 175° → wrong direction ✗\n```\n\n---\n\n**What it needs from each object:**\n```json\n{\n  \"id\": \"furn-sofa-01\",\n  \"orientation\": [0, 1],    ← facing vector\n  \"target_id\": \"furn-tv-01\" ← what it should face\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n**Why it's optional:**\nNot every object needs to face something specific. A storage rack doesn't care about orientation. A machine control panel does. The Space Type Agent decides which objects need orientation checking.\n\n---\n\n**For your industrial layout:**\n- Workstations → must face the machine they operate\n- Conveyor sections → must align with flow direction\n- QC tables → must face the product flow\n\n---\n\nWant to build it now or skip to collision?",

A machine placed perfectly for visibility might block the main circulation path. The agent negotiates between competing spatial priorities based on the space type and user profile.\n\n---\n\n**The output:**\nA scored, optimized layout with a spatial argument — not just \"here's where everything goes\" but \"here's why this configuration works better than the alternatives.\"\n\n---\n\n**The scale:**\nAbstract enough to work anywhere — a kitchen, a workshop, a factory, a living room. The base layout is never touched again.\n\n---\n\n**2 — `save_session(layout_data, workspace_path)`**\nOverwrites `workspace/session_active.json` with the current state of the layout. Called every time an object is added, moved, or analyzed. Always the latest version.\n\n---\n\n**3 — `close_session(workspace_path, output_path, layout_name)`**\nCalled when the user approves at the checkpoint. Does three things:\n- Writes the final layout to `output/[layout_name]_[timestamp]_final.json`\n- Deletes `workspace/session_active.json`\n- Returns the output path so the agent can confirm it\n\n---\n\n**4 — `detect_existing_session(workspace_path)`**\nCalled at bootstrap startup. Checks if `session_active.json` already exists from a previous crashed or incomplete session. Returns `True` or `False` so bootstrap can ask the user what to do.\n\n---\n\n**Tell Claude Code:**\n\n```\nCreate _runtime/session.py\n\nA utility module for managing the workspace \nsession lifecycle. No LangGraph dependency.\nAdd clear comments to every function.\n\nFunction 1: create_session(base_layout_path, workspace_path)\n- Creates workspace/ folder if it doesn't exist\n- Copies base layout JSON to workspace/session_active.json\n- Returns the loaded layout dict\n- Comment: explain this is the starting point, \n  base layout is never modified\n\nFunction 2: save_session(layout_data, workspace_path)\n- Overwrites workspace/session_active.json with \n  current layout dict (pretty printed JSON)\n- Comment: explain this is called after every \n  object placement or analysis update\n\nFunction 3: close_session(workspace_path, output_path, layout_name)\n- Creates output/ folder if it doesn't exist\n- Writes final layout to:\n  output/[layout_name]_[timestamp]_final.json\n  timestamp format: YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM\n- Deletes workspace/session_active.json\n- Returns the output file path as string\n- Comment: explain this is called only when \n  user approves at the checkpoint\n\nFunction 4: detect_existing_session(workspace_path)\n- Returns True if workspace/session_active.json exists\n- Returns False if not\n- Comment: explain bootstrap uses this to ask \n  user to resume or start fresh\n\nAll paths: use pathlib.Path\nAll JSON: use json with indent=2 and utf-8\nNo external dependencies beyond stdlib\nShow code before writing.\n```",

Same style as \nsession.py.\n\nHere is the target result — write this exactly:\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\nimport argparse\nimport json\nimport os\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass\nfrom pathlib import Path\nfrom typing import Any\nfrom _runtime.config import load_settings\nfrom _runtime.mcp_client import McpClient\nfrom _runtime.llm import create_chat_llm, get_llm_response_format\nfrom _runtime.session import detect_existing_session, create_session\n\n\n@dataclass\nclass Context:\n    \"\"\"Everything the agent graph needs to run — passed from main.py into graph.py.\"\"\"\n    llm: Any\n    mcp_client: McpClient\n    tools: list[dict[str, Any]]\n    layout_data: dict[str, Any]\n    max_iterations: int\n    workspace_path: Path\n    output_path: Path\n    layout_name: str\n\n\ndef bootstrap() -> Context:\n    \"\"\"Load settings, connect to MCP, discover tools, build LLM, and start session.\n\n    Call this once from main.py and pass the returned Context into run_agent().\n    Reads --layout <name> from the command line or LAYOUT_FILE env variable.\n    \"\"\"\n    settings = load_settings()\n\n    # Resolve team_03/ root — two levels up from this file\n    # (_runtime/bootstrap.py → python/ → team_03/)\n    team_dir = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2]\n\n    # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n    # Resolve layout name from --layout CLI arg or LAYOUT_FILE env variable.\n    # CLI takes priority. Env variable allows VS Code launch configs to set it\n    # without typing --layout every run.\n    # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n    _parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(add_help=False)\n    _parser.add_argument(\"--layout\", default=None)\n    _known, _ = _parser.parse_known_args()\n\n    if not _known.layout:\n        env_layout = os.environ.get(\"LAYOUT_FILE\", \"\").strip()\n        if env_layout:\n            _known.layout = env_layout.replace(\".json\", \"\")\n        else:\n            raise ValueError(\n                \"Missing --layout argument. Provide a layout name, e.g. --layout industrial_005\"\n            )\n\n    # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n    # Find the layout JSON anywhere under layout/ subfolders.\n    # rglob handles nested folders like layout/industrial_100/industrial_005.json\n    # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n    matches = list((team_dir / \"layout\").rglob(f\"{_known.layout}.json\"))\n    if not matches:\n        raise FileNotFoundError(\n            f\"Layout '{_known.layout}.json' not found anywhere under layout/\"\n        )\n    resolved_layout = matches[0]\n    layout_name = _known.layout\n\n    # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n    # Define workspace and output paths.\n    # workspace/ holds the live session file — overwritten on every update.\n    # output/ holds the final approved layouts — timestamped, never overwritten.\n    # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n    workspace_path = team_dir / \"workspace\"\n    output_path = team_dir / \"output\"\n\n    # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n    # Session management — check if a previous session exists.\n    # If yes: ask the user to resume or start fresh.\n    # If no: create a new session by copying the base layout to workspace/.\n    # The base layout is NEVER modified — all changes go to session_active.json.\n    # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n    if detect_existing_session(workspace_path):\n        print(f\"\\nExisting session found for a previous run.\")\n        print(\"Resume existing session? (y/n): \", end=\"\")\n        answer = input().strip().lower()\n\n        if answer == \"y\":\n            # Resume — load the last saved state from the workspace file\n            session_file = workspace_path / \"session_active.json\"\n            layout_data = json.loads(session_file.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\"))\n            print(f\"Resuming session from: {session_file}\")\n        else:\n            # Fresh start — overwrite workspace with base layout\n            layout_data = create_session(resolved_layout, workspace_path)\n            print(f\"Starting fresh session from: {resolved_layout.name}\")\n    else:\n        # No existing session — create one from the base layout\n        layout_data = create_session(resolved_layout, workspace_path)\n        print(f\"New session started from: {resolved_layout.name}\")\n\n    # Connect to the Grasshopper MCP server and discover available tools\n    mcp_client = McpClient(settings.mcp_endpoint, settings.request_timeout_seconds)\n    mcp_client.initialize()\n    tools = mcp_client.list_tools()\n    print(f\"Discovered MCP tools: {[t.get('name') for t in tools]}\")\n\n    # Build the LLM with a structured-output schema tailored to available tools\n    llm = create_chat_llm(\n        api_key=settings.api_key,\n        base_url=settings.base_url,\n        llm_model=settings.llm_model,\n        timeout_seconds=settings.request_timeout_seconds,\n        model_kwargs=get_llm_response_format(tools),\n    )\n\n    return Context(\n        llm=llm,\n        mcp_client=mcp_client,\n        tools=tools,\n        layout_data=layout_data,\n        max_iterations=settings.max_iterations,\n        workspace_path=workspace_path,\n        output_path=output_path,\n        layout_name=layout_name,\n    )\n```",
                    "citations": []
                }

1) Filtrar argumentos: quitar nulls\n            raw_args = {k: v for k, v in call[\"arguments\"].items() if v is not None and v != \"\"}\n\n            # 2) Whitelist por tool: solo dejar los parametros validos\n            allowed_params = TOOL_SCHEMAS.get(tool_name, set())\n            tool_args = {k: v for k, v in raw_args.items() if k in allowed_params}\n\n            # 3) Inyectar layout_json solo si el tool lo necesita\n            if tool_name in TOOLS_NEEDING_LAYOUT:\n                tool_args[\"layout_json\"] = state[\"layout_json_string\"]\n\n            print(f\"Calling tool: {tool_name} with arguments (cleaned): {list(tool_args.keys())}\")\n\n            # 4) Llamar al tool\n            tool_output = mcp_client.call_tool(tool_name, tool_args)\n\n            # 5) Guardar resultado\n            write_tool_result(tool_output, edited_layout_path)\n\n            # 6) Si vino layout actualizado, refrescar state\n            try:\n                updated = json.loads(tool_output.strip())\n                if isinstance(updated, dict):\n                    if \"rooms\" in updated:\n                        state[\"layout_json_string\"] = json.dumps(updated)\n                    elif \"doors\" in updated:\n                        # 7) Anadir a historial\n            state[\"messages\"].append({\n                \"role\": \"assistant\",\n                \"content\": json.dumps({\n                    \"action\": \"tool\",\n                    \"final_response\": \"\",\n                    \"tool_calls\": [{\"name\": tool_name, \"arguments\": {k: v for k, v in tool_args.items() if k != \"layout_json\"}}],\n                }),\n            })\n\n            state[\"messages\"].append({\n                \"role\": \"user\",\n                \"content\": f\"Tool result: {tool_output}\",\n            })\n\n            print(f\"Tool result: {tool_output[:500]}\")  # Recortar log para no saturar terminal\n\n        state[\"pending_tool_calls\"] = None\n        return state\n\n    return tool_node\n```\n\nthis is the tool.py that i pulled and there's some modification in it. i have the main tools.py from the initial teacher file i will add it please see everything and let me know what should i do if i should stick to the main one or this version now or what.\ni will send now the initial one",

                    "text": "```python\nfrom __future__ import annotations\nimport json\nfrom pathlib import Path\nfrom typing import Any\nfrom _runtime.llm import write_tool_result\n\n\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n# Tool node — executes MCP tool calls requested by the reason node.\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\ndef build_tool_node(mcp_client, allowed_tools, edited_layout_path):\n    \"\"\"Return a tool node function ready to be added to a LangGraph StateGraph.\"\"\"\n\n    allowed_names = {t[\"name\"] for t in allowed_tools if t.get(\"name\")}\n\n    def tool_node(state):\n\n        # Iterate over the pending tool calls\n        for call in state[\"pending_tool_calls\"]:\n\n            # Stop the process if max number of iterations is reached\n            state[\"iteration\"] += 1\n            if state[\"iteration\"] > state[\"max_iterations\"]:\n                raise RuntimeError(\"Max iterations exceeded\")\n\n\n            # Get the tool name and check its valid\n            tool_name = call[\"name\"]\n            if tool_name not in allowed_names:\n                raise RuntimeError(f\"Tool '{tool_name}' is not in the allowed tools list\")\n            \n            print(f\"Calling tool: {tool_name} with arguments: {call['arguments']}\")\n\n            # Cleanup any null values accidentally included by the LLM\n            tool_args = {k: v for k, v in call[\"arguments\"].items() if v is not None}\n\n            # Inject layout_json\n            if \"layout_json\" in tool_args:\n                tool_args[\"layout_json\"] = state[\"layout_json_string\"]\n\n            # Call the tool\n            tool_output = mcp_client.call_tool(tool_name, tool_args)\n\n            # Store the updated layout returned by the MCP tool to a json file\n            write_tool_result(tool_output, edited_layout_path)\n\n            # If the tool returned valid JSON, update the layout in state so\n            # subsequent tool calls in this loop receive the latest layout.\n            try:\n                updated = json.loads(tool_output.strip())\n                if isinstance(updated, dict):\n                    state[\"layout_json_string\"] = json.dumps(updated)\n            except (json.JSONDecodeError, AttributeError):\n                pass\n\n            # Append the tool call and its result to the conversation history\n            state[\"messages\"].append({\n                \"role\": \"assistant\",\n                \"content\": json.dumps({\n                    \"action\": \"tool\",\n                    \"final_response\": \"\",\n                    \"tool_calls\": [{\"name\": tool_name, \"arguments\": tool_args}],\n                }),\n            })\n            \n            state[\"messages\"].append({\n                \"role\": \"user\",\n                \"content\": f\"Tool result: {tool_output}\",\n            })\n            print(f\"Tool result: {tool_output}\")\n\n        state[\"pending_tool_calls\"] = None\n        return state\n\n    return tool_node\n\n```",

Parse incoming JSON from MCP\n    raw = json_str.ToString() if hasattr(json_str, 'ToString') else str(json_str)\n    args = json.loads(raw)\n    placed = args.get(\"placed\", [])\n\n    # Colors for drawing\n    edge_color  = System.Drawing.Color.FromArgb(255, 40, 120, 60)\n    label_color = System.Drawing.Color.White\n\n    # Draw all placed objects\n    disp = rd.CustomDisplay(True)\n\n    for obj in placed:\n        x0, y0 = obj[\"position\"]\n        fw, fd, fh = obj[\"size\"]\n\n        # Create 3D box from bottom-left corner\n        pt    = rg.Point3d(x0, y0, 0)\n        plane = rg.Plane(pt, rg.Vector3d.ZAxis)\n        box   = rg.Box(\n            plane,\n            rg.Interval(0, fw),\n            rg.Interval(0, fd),\n            rg.Interval(0, fh)\n        )\n        brep = box.ToBrep()\n        if brep:\n            for b_edge in brep.Edges:\n                disp.AddCurve(b_edge.EdgeCurve, edge_color, 2)\n\n        # Add text label above the box\n        cx = x0 + fw / 2.0\n        cy = y0 + fd / 2.0\n        lp = rg.Plane(rg.Point3d(cx, cy, fh + 0.05), rg.Vector3d.ZAxis)\n        try:\n            t3d = rd.Text3d(obj[\"name\"], lp, 0.12)\n            t3d.HorizontalAlignment = rd.TextHorizontalAlignment.Center\n            disp.AddText(t3d, label_color)\n        except Exception:\n            pass\n\n    # Store display and redraw viewport\n    sc.sticky[STICKY_KEY] = disp\n    Rhino.RhinoDoc.ActiveDoc.Views.Redraw()\n\n    # Return success to MCP\n    a = json.dumps({\n        \"status\": \"ok\",\n        \"drawn\": len(placed)\n    })\n\nexcept Exception as e:\n    a = json.dumps({\"error\": str(e)})\n```\n\n---\n\nPaste this into the GH Python Script component that handles `place_objects` in your Result Cluster. Colors for drawing\n    edge_color  = System.Drawing.Color.FromArgb(255, 40, 120, 60)\n    label_color = System.Drawing.Color.White\n\n    # Draw all placed objects as 3D boxes\n    disp = rd.CustomDisplay(True)\n\n    for obj in placed:\n        x0, y0 = obj[\"position\"]\n        fw, fd, fh = obj[\"size\"]\n\n        # Create 3D box from bottom-left corner at ground level\n        pt    = rg.Point3d(x0, y0, 0)\n        plane = rg.Plane(pt, rg.Vector3d.ZAxis)\n        box   = rg.Box(\n            plane,\n            rg.Interval(0, fw),\n            rg.Interval(0, fd),\n            rg.Interval(0, fh)\n        )\n        brep = box.ToBrep()\n        if brep:\n            for b_edge in brep.Edges:\n                disp.AddCurve(b_edge.EdgeCurve, edge_color, 2)\n\n        # Add text label above the box\n        cx = x0 + fw / 2.0\n        cy = y0 + fd / 2.0\n        lp = rg.Plane(rg.Point3d(cx, cy, fh + 0.05), rg.Vector3d.ZAxis)\n        try:\n            t3d = rd.Text3d(obj[\"name\"], lp, 0.12)\n            t3d.HorizontalAlignment = rd.TextHorizontalAlignment.Center\n            disp.AddText(t3d, label_color)\n        except Exception:\n            pass\n\n    # Store display and redraw viewport\n    sc.sticky[STICKY_KEY] = disp\n    Rhino.RhinoDoc.ActiveDoc.Views.Redraw()\n\n    # Return success to MCP with count of drawn objects\n    a = json.dumps({\n        \"status\": \"ok\",\n        \"drawn\": len(placed)\n    })\n\nexcept Exception as e:\n    a = json.dumps({\"error\": str(e)})\n```\n\nPaste this into your GH Python Script component then run the test again.",

Any]]) -> dict[str, Any]:\n    merged_properties: dict[str, Any] = {}\n    for tool in tools:\n        input_schema = tool.get(\"inputSchema\")\n        if not isinstance(input_schema, dict):\n            continue\n        properties = input_schema.get(\"properties\")\n        if not isinstance(properties, dict):\n            continue\n        for property_name, property_schema in ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n# LLM response parsing helpers\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\ndef _strip_markdown_code_fence(content: str) -> str:\n    stripped = content.strip()\n    if not stripped.startswith(\"```\"):\n        return stripped\n    lines = stripped.splitlines()\n    if len(lines) < 3:\n        return stripped\n    if not lines[-1].strip().startswith(\"```\"):\n        return stripped\n    return \"\\n\".join(lines[1:-1]).strip()\n\n\ndef _parse_llm_json(content: str) -> dict[str, Any]:\n    content_stripped = content.strip()\n\n    # Handle Claude's <function_calls> XML format\n    if \"<function_calls>\" in content_stripped:\n        match = re.search(r'<function_calls>(.*?)</function_calls>', content_stripped, re.DOTALL)\n        if match:\n            calls_text = match.group(1).strip()\n            try:\n                calls = json.loads(calls_text)\n                if isinstance(calls, list):\n                    return {\n                        \"action\": \"tool\",\n                        \"final_response\": \"\",\n                        \"tool_calls\": [\n                            {\n                                \"name\": c[\"name\"],\n                                \"arguments\": c.get(\"arguments\", c.get(\"parameters\", {}))\n                            }\n                            for c in calls\n                        ]\n                    }\n            except json.JSONDecodeError:\n                pass\n\n    # Extract JSON from ```json ... ``` fence anywhere in content\n    fence_match = re.search(r'```(?:json)?\\s*(\\{.*?\\})\\s*```', content_stripped, re.DOTALL)\n    if fence_match:\n        try:\n            parsed = json.loads(fence_match.group(1))\n            if isinstance(parsed, dict):\n                return parsed\n        except json.JSONDecodeError:\n            pass\n\n    # Find raw JSON object anywhere in content\n    json_match = re.search(r'\\{.*\\}', content_stripped, re.DOTALL)\n    if json_match:\n        try:\n            parsed = json.loads(json_match.group(0))\n            if isinstance(parsed, dict):\n                return parsed\n        except json.JSONDecodeError:\n            pass\n\n    # Try direct parse — pure JSON response with no surrounding text\n    try:\n        parsed = json.loads(_strip_markdown_code_fence(content_stripped))\n        if not isinstance(parsed, dict):\n            raise RuntimeError(\"LLM JSON response must be an object\")\n        return parsed\n    except json.JSONDecodeError:\n        pass\n\n    raise RuntimeError(\n        \"Could not extract JSON from LLM response: {}\".format(content_stripped[:200])\n    )\n\n\ndef _normalize_llm_decision(parsed: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:\n    action = parsed.get(\"action\")\n\n    if action == \"final\":\n        return {\"action\": \"final\", \"final_response\": parsed[\"final_response\"]}\n\n    if action == \"tool\":\n        tool_calls = parsed.get(\"tool_calls\")\n        if not isinstance(tool_calls, list) or not tool_calls:\n            raise RuntimeError(\"LLM tool decision must include a non-empty 'tool_calls' array\")\n        return {\n            \"action\": \"tool\",\n            \"tool_calls\": [{\"name\": t[\"name\"], \"arguments\": t[\"arguments\"]} for t in tool_calls],\n        }\n\n    if \"final_response\" in parsed:\n        return {\"action\": \"final\", \"tool_calls\": [{\"name\": tool_call[\"name\"], \"arguments\": tool_call[\"arguments\"]}],\n        }\n\n    tool_calls = parsed.get(\"tool_calls\")\n    if isinstance(tool_calls, list) and tool_calls:\n        return {\n            \"action\": \"tool\",\n            \"tool_calls\": [{\"name\": t[\"name\"], \"arguments\": t[\"arguments\"]} for t in tool_calls],\n        }\n\n    raise RuntimeError(\"LLM response must include either 'final_response' or 'tool_call'\")\n\n\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n# Anthropic-specific call — bypasses LangChain entirely\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\ndef _call_anthropic(\n    client_dict: dict,\n    system_prompt: str,\n    messages:
